title: What to Do on the Sabbath
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: What to Do on the Sabbath - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt
outline: Introduction: Nearing the end of the Sabbath series
What God commands us to do on the Lord's Day
Rest from ordinary labor and recreations
The whole day given to public and private worship
Works of necessity and mercy on the Sabbath
Practical application for families and individuals
Conclusion: The Sabbath as preparation for eternal rest
summary: Pastor Tito Lyro continues the Sunday School series on WCF Chapter 21 near its conclusion, focusing on what Christians are called to do on the Lord's Day. He explains the confessional teaching that the Sabbath is to be observed by resting from ordinary labors and devoting the day to public and private worship.
transcript:
We are probably one or two lessons away from finishing our series on chapter 21 of our Confession of Faith regarding the Sabbath day. Our last lesson was four weeks ago because of the various Christmas programs and special Sundays. Four weeks ago we finished looking at Sunday as the Christian Sabbath — we saw that that is exactly what the New Testament teaches: from the Sabbath day under the old covenant, in the new covenant we moved to the first day of the week, celebrating the Lord's resurrection. And so that is where we are. Today we come back to the confession, paragraph 8, which talks about what to do on the Sabbath day.

## Introduction: Why We Resist the Sabbath

Let me start by saying this: when people look at the subject of the Sabbath, they immediately get upset. That seems to be the most common reaction — "Here he goes again, telling me what I can and can't do with my day off." If that is the first thought that comes to your mind, let's start by repenting of selfishness. Every day, every hour, belongs to the Lord — and that is how we have to view every single day, including the Sabbath day.

Second, let's remember that God gave us ten words. He could have said all kinds of things, but He listed ten things — the top ten things He wants us to pay attention to, and this is one of them. Yet we tend to disregard it. We look at the first commandment — "have no other gods before the God of the Bible" — and we say, yes, that's important. The second commandment, about images, we think about sometimes. The third commandment, don't take the Lord's name in vain — we think that means only using the words "God" or "Jesus" carelessly. And then the fourth commandment we just don't care for, it seems. But we are really invested in the last six, right? We don't want our children to disobey us. We don't want our spouse to cheat on us. We don't want to be killed. We don't want to be lied to. We don't want our stuff stolen. We're very committed to those six.

The last six regulate things in the temporal, horizontal, physical world. The first four regulate things for eternity. There will be no marriage in eternity. There will be no parenting in eternity. There will be no possessions in eternity. All those things governed by the last six will be gone. But the first four remain for eternity — and we tend to disregard them.

Why do you think we naturally disregard the first four and emphasize the last six? At the end of the day, we are self-centered creatures. The last six commandments are about *me*, at least that's how we see them: forbidding my kids from disobeying me, keeping my wife from cheating on me, keeping my things from being stolen, keeping my life from being taken. Me, me, me. And often we take that same approach to the fourth commandment — "there they go, taking things from me" — as if having a day set apart for the Lord is somehow a great burden.

And yet, 1 John tells us that for those who truly believe in Jesus, the commandments of God are not a burden. That is the view of a person of basic faith in Christ when we talk about the fourth commandment.

Any questions or thoughts?

*[Scott observes that the Sabbath is one of the commandments we disregard most readily, yet it's one of the most significant throughout the Old Testament — its violation was part of what led to Israel's captivity.]*

Not only in the Old Testament, but Jesus went out of His way to confront people on the Sabbath — not to do away with it, but to recalibrate how people thought about it. Hiding behind "I'm a New Testament Christian, this isn't for me" is a really lazy, unthinking way of dealing with the Sabbath, because the Sabbath is all over the New Testament. If you don't want to grapple with the Bible, that's your loss — but it is all over the New Testament. So don't hide behind that lazy, unthinking approach.

## Westminster Confession 21.8: What to Do on the Sabbath

Look at page 861 of the hymnal, paragraph 8 of chapter 21, which talks about the Sabbath commandment. It says this:

> *"This Sabbath is then kept holy unto the Lord, when men, after a due preparing of their hearts, and ordering of their common affairs beforehand, do not only observe a holy rest all the day from their own works, words, and thoughts about their worldly employments and recreations, but also are taken up the whole time in the public and private exercises of His worship, and in the duties of necessity and mercy."*

This tells us several things about the Sabbath.

### It Is an Entire Day

The first is that the Sabbath is an entire day. The very word of the commandment requires an entire day. In Exodus 20, God speaking through Moses says:

> *"Remember the Sabbath day, to keep it holy. Six days you shall labor and do all your work, but the seventh day is the Sabbath of the Lord your God."* (Exodus 20:8–10)

The Sabbath day is contrasted with the other six days. There is no way that the word "day" means an hour from 11 to noon — exegetically, it just doesn't work that way. The command talks about a whole day.

Now, it does not require that the Sabbath begin at the evening of the previous day, because the Scriptures don't prescribe that. The Gospel of John uses the Roman reckoning of time — similar to what we use today, with a 24-hour period — and the important thing is that it is a whole day. And it is not the day that *we* pick. You can go to the Gospel Coalition and find articles about "Sabbathing" on whatever day you want. But the Scriptures clearly tell us it is the first day of the week — not Tuesday, not Wednesday, not every other Monday, but Sunday, the Lord's Day. The entire day is to be kept holy — set aside, set apart, separated from the rest.

Any questions on that? *[Question about the length of a day — 24 hours.]* Yes, 24 hours.

### It Involves Preparation

The confession says "after a due preparing of their hearts, and ordering of their common affairs beforehand" — so the Sabbath involves preparation. Just as with anything else in life, we need to be prepared to keep the Sabbath. We prepare ourselves to go on vacation. We prepare ourselves when buying a car or a house. We prepare ourselves for the work week. We also need to prepare ourselves for the Lord's Day. Preparation is both spiritual — preparing our hearts — and physical — ordering common affairs beforehand.

If you say, "I have too many things; there is no way I can do everything in six days" — that means you are doing more than what the Lord is calling you to do. If you cannot do what you need to do in the hours allotted in the six days of labor, it means you are trying to do more than what God calls you to do. You need to figure out how to divest yourself of some of those things so that you can do what God calls you to do in those days.

Arcibald Alexander, a Southern Presbyterian minister, wrote this concerning his experience of preparation for the Sabbath:

> *"How sacredly was the Sabbath improved. My father went about making the best of the sacred day just as seriously and systematically as any wise businessman planning to put in the best work possible on some favorable day in the middle of harvest. He acted on this clear, rational, and conscientious conviction: I have a great and urgent work to do for my own soul and for others. The one day in seven which a kind Heavenly Father has endeavored to secure for me for this task is none too much if improved the best. So I must make the most of it."*

That is the idea — the father preparing the family for the day. There is a spiritual preparation for Sunday, for the Sabbath day, for the day of worship. It can be done Saturday night or Sunday morning. And it should really begin with thankfulness for the Lord's Day. It is a good day, not a burden. As we prepare for it, we should start with gratitude that a good God gives us this gift.

In that preparation, we should spend some time in self-examination: Where are we with the Lord? What has the Lord done for us? How can we serve Him? What blessings have we experienced? We see this in question 171 of the Larger Catechism, which talks about how we prepare ourselves for the Lord's Supper — and since we observe the Lord's Supper every Sunday, these things go into that preparation as well. Meditation, relationships, and so on. God gives us the opportunity every week to do a bit of self-examination, to be reminded who we are in Christ.

In that spiritual preparation, we should also seek to stir up our affections so that we can come to church ready to adore and praise God. If you have played sports at a competitive level, you know that on game day there is always a rally — not an artificial hype, but a stirring up of excitement so that you can be at your best. Something like that is needed for worship: stirring up our affections in preparation for the Word.

Any questions or comments on that?

As we prepare ourselves spiritually, we should also seek God in prayer. Pray for His aid as the congregation gathers for worship. Pray that God would feed our pastor so that he in turn can feed you. Pray that the Lord would bless the preaching of His Word, that He would be working in your heart to receive His Word. Pray for the Sunday School teachers as they prepare their lessons. Pray that distractions would be removed from your mind and from our midst.

We humans have a tendency to show up at church, drop into the pew, having filled our lives with noise all week, and then expect to somehow be transported to the doors of heaven by someone else's effort — and then we leave upset when that doesn't happen, blaming somebody else. We need to prepare ourselves spiritually in order to enjoy the Lord's Day and the worship of the Lord.

Joel Beeke says: "We ought to be in prayer during the week for the proclamation of the Word and the pastor, that the pastor will preach in the power of the Holy Spirit. In our more immediate preparation for the Lord's Day, we should pray that the Word of God will be preached with great power, that God Himself will speak to us and to each person in the congregation through the act of preaching."

The best way to make the sermon better for you is to pray for those who are going to be teaching — Sunday School, morning service, afternoon service. Pray that the Lord would prepare your heart to receive His Word. Pray together as a family before you leave for church. It doesn't have to be extensive. In our family, as we'd sit in the car, we would say a prayer for the day together, asking the Lord to sanctify it. If you know what passage is being preached through, read it ahead of time so that you can be ready to receive it.

Any questions or comments on the spiritual preparation?

### Physical Preparation

There is also physical preparation. Tie up the loose ends — use Saturday to complete work and the major responsibilities of the week. When done, don't think about them until Monday. It is a great thing that God gives you permission to set all that aside. The Creator of the universe is saying that the universe will survive without your having to worry about your work for 24 hours. When we say "no, that's not true" — guess what we're making ourselves? We're making ourselves the Lord of the universe. It is not a matter of diligence; it is a matter of pride and idolatry when we do that.

Plan time on Saturday to finish chores that cannot be done during the week. As I said earlier, if you cannot complete your work in six days, you have taken more than God intends for you to take. Observing the Sabbath is like tithing. We know we must give one day to the Lord, so we plan to do everything in the other days — as a recognition that every day belongs to the Lord.

For physical preparation: make sure there's gas in the car before Sunday. Have the groceries you need for Sunday already bought. And here is the one that will provoke the most pushback:

*[Alex answers: go to bed early.]*

Yes — go to bed early on Saturday. Have you noticed that you are more tired on Sunday than on the other days of the week? Sometimes we think Saturday was made to stay out late. It is interesting that we are careful to go to bed on time when we have to go to work or school, but not for the worship of God. That's the one thing that doesn't matter, apparently. "I can stay up until whatever hour of the morning." We don't do that for other things. And remember, it's not what we say that demonstrates what we think is important — it's how we use our time, our money, and our resources. Plan Saturday evening so that you can be in bed in time to get the sleep you require. And sometimes we are tired on Sunday because we exert ourselves too much on Saturday — going out and running a marathon when you haven't exercised all week, and then Sunday you can't function.

Look at Westminster Larger Catechism question 119, which lists the sins that are forbidden by the fourth commandment:

> *"The sins forbidden in the fourth commandment are, all omissions of the duties required, all careless, negligent, and unfruitful performance of them, and being weary of them; all profaning the day, all the day by idleness, and doing that which is in itself sinful, and by all needless works, words, and thoughts about our worldly employments and recreations."*

You can see that there is a call to great diligence in that day. And the basis of the cessation of work is God Himself ceasing from the work of creation and redemption. All the work of creation has been done; all the work of redemption has been done — and thus the Sabbath day represents that.

Any questions before we continue?

*[Jerry raises the question of occupations that require people to work on the Sabbath.]*

I want to challenge the notion that there are as many of those as we think. By the way, the state of Washington has a law that a lot of people don't know about: an employer cannot require you to work on Sunday if you have a religious objection to it. That doesn't apply to the military or first responders, but that is a very small minority of the population. I'll keep talking so we can get to that subject toward the end of the lesson.

## The Rest of the Lord's Day

As we think about rest on the Sabbath, I don't think we should think of it as a day of inactivity. Rest is a cessation from daily work, but it is not inactivity. We are doing all of this: public worship, private worship, rest, hospitality, ministry — all these things are involved on the Lord's Day. The confession says we are to be taken up the whole time in the public and private exercises of His worship and in the duties of necessity and mercy.

We can see the relationship between corporate worship and the Sabbath in Psalm 92. And we have seen that Psalm 92 speaks of several public meetings in a day for worship — morning and evening. So the Sabbath day is marked by corporate worship, celebration, anticipation, and joyful gathering. That is why we gather to worship the Lord on the first day of the week, the Sabbath day — and it was also the practice of the apostolic church as we have seen.

But it is also a time for private worship, not just here at church but also at home. If you stay here for all the services on Sunday — Sunday School, morning service, lunch, and afternoon service — you have been here for about five to six hours. What about the rest of the day? Some of that time should be dedicated to private worship. Families worshiping the Lord at home.

What are some things we can do to worship God privately? These are suggestions — use your imagination, but these are things I have seen practiced, and that my own family has practiced over the years.

**Use the time meaningfully.** Do things that are truly beneficial and worshipful. For example, take time to review the sermon. Pretty much all our Sunday lessons are posted by around 8:15 PM, depending on how late the afternoon service runs — Sunday School, morning sermon, and afternoon sermon are all posted most Sundays. So you have time to review the sermon afterward, whether by taking notes during the service or listening again at home.

**Take time to sing and pray as a family.** Maybe at night before you put the kids to bed, pray and sing with them. Usually we don't have time to do that during the week because we are so busy. Here we have a day given to us where we can actually breathe and do those things.

And now here is the one where all the amens will come: **take a nap.** Rest. Right? Sabbath rest. After church. The confession actually does not list resting as a Sabbath activity, which might seem contradictory — but resting is indeed part of what you should do on the Lord's Day. The Bible does not forbid napping; the Bible encourages rest. That said, going home and sleeping for six hours may not be in the spirit of the Sabbath. But rest is good.

**Catch up on spiritual things you didn't have time for during the week.** Sunday is a gift from God for us to do those things we didn't have the chance to do during the week. Read a good Christian book. Catch up on Bible reading. Take time to pray. Read a good Christian magazine. Watch or scroll through whatever edifying videos you like. Memorize Scripture. Study the catechisms. These are all things that can be done on the Lord's Day.

**Hospitality.** Besides private worship with your own family, you can use Sunday for hospitality. It doesn't have to be fancy — it can be rice and beans, or any other simple food from your own ethnic tradition. Just invite people over. Whatever you have, serve it. Spend time together. Talk about the Sunday sermon. Talk about the Sunday School lessons. Sing together. Pray for each other. Encourage one another. Read the Bible together. Find out how people are doing. Get to know each other. This is all part of spiritual Sabbath observance.

**Ministry.** Another thing you can do is use Sunday for ministry. Visit someone. Visit the elderly. Visit the sick. Witness in a public place. Pass out tracts. Visit people who are alone. Do door-to-door evangelism. Do counseling. Ministry can be done on the Sabbath — the Lord's Day — as well.

Why am I listing all these things? When you focus on what you *should* do and *could* do on the Lord's Day, there isn't much room to focus on what you shouldn't do. Instead of thinking "these are all the things I can't do today," think: "Look at God's goodness — He gives me a day where I can put everything else aside and focus on all these things that I don't have time for the other days of the week."

In my mind, a lot of the opposition to this doctrine of the Sabbath comes from an unwillingness to recognize the blessing of the day and to give it up to God. Yet God promised that this day would be a delight for us. In Isaiah 58, He says:

> *"If you turn away your foot from the Sabbath, from doing your pleasure on My holy day, and call the Sabbath a delight, the holy day of the Lord honorable, and shall honor Him, not doing your own ways, nor finding your own pleasure, nor speaking your own words, then you shall delight yourself in the Lord; and I will cause you to ride on the high hills of the earth, and feed you with the heritage of Jacob your father. The mouth of the Lord has spoken."* (Isaiah 58:13–14)

If you call the Sabbath a delight, it will be a delight to you. And if you say, "but that's talking about Israel" — well, if you're going to use that argument for Isaiah 58, you have to discard Jesus as your Messiah. Why? Because Isaiah 58 is a continuation of Isaiah 53, which talks about the ministry of Jesus as the substitute for your sins. So if Isaiah 53 is for you, then Isaiah 58 is for you as well — because it is the same context, the same narrative, the same lesson God is teaching through the prophet.

Any questions or comments before we continue?

## A Word to Families with Young Children

It is way easier to keep the Sabbath in this manner when you don't have children. What I want to say is this: we have to be careful — and this was a struggle for us as we raised our own kids — that we don't alienate our children in how we observe the Sabbath. If we make it the worst day of the week for them, the most boring day, and if we are inconsistent on top of that, we will alienate them. If we end up alienating our children by the way we keep the Sabbath, we are not keeping the Sabbath — we are doing something else. We need to teach them regularly about the Sabbath. Just as we need to be prepared as adults, they need to be prepared as children. If we have a poor attitude about Sunday, they will have a poor attitude about it as well. But if they understand the blessing that the Sabbath is, they will generally have no trouble keeping it.

As you come to public worship with your children — and we will spend more time on this in a future lesson — have you ever taken the time to explain to your children how to worship? Take the bulletin home and say, "This is what we did this, and this is why." They don't have to be teenagers for you to do that. We need to teach them about public worship.

This is very important: children should participate in public worship. This is why we don't have junior church at the 11 o'clock service. We want our children to be part of our service. Joel Beeke says: "If we believe that in corporate worship we come into God's presence in a unique way, and that preaching is the primary means of grace because in it we hear the living voice of God, we will resist depriving our children of this privilege."

If Christ were on the earth and you had the choice of letting your children hear Him teach or sending them off to children's church, what would you choose? We hear the voice of Christ when the Word of God is being proclaimed. That is one of the reasons we really encourage families to worship together on the Lord's Day. I think it is generally a good thing for families to sit together in the worship service. That is why we have an age limit of three in our nursery — we don't want kids to stay there too long.

Any questions on that?

**Teach your children to behave in church.** Many families with middle school, teenage, and high school kids who sit attentively at the front have put in a great deal of work to get there. Their children were regenerated early by the Lord, and the Spirit was working in them — great credit to them. But there was also a great deal of spanking and explaining. Emily worked with our children constantly, even while I was up here and they were down there. It is hard work. It feels like you are not moving anywhere. It feels like doing the same things over and over and over again. But the growth is there when you look back over the years.

Even while you are so busy trying to get your child to pay attention or stop talking, even when you haven't heard a whole sermon in three years — that in itself is an act of worship. There is a mystical, gracious presence of the Spirit as you do that. Because as a believer, you are training your children for heaven. It doesn't matter if they can't throw a ball. It doesn't matter if their table manners aren't great. It doesn't matter if they don't get into the right college. What matters is that you are training them for heaven. And the closest we can get to heaven on this earth is the public worship of the Lord with the saints on the Lord's Day.

One specific way to help children is to get them settled down *before* the service begins — not dragging them by the ankle at five minutes to eleven. Get them settled before the service. Also, those without children, or with older children, should be patient with those who are in the thick of training their children, and rejoice that the Lord has given so many children to our church.

I read something this week that said the signs of a church in which the Spirit is working are the sound of small children in the service and the voice of men singing. Those are two marks of a vibrant church.

Teach children to use the restroom before the service, not during. Though in our church, adults seem to have a harder time with that — I don't understand it. You can sit through a three-hour movie without getting up, but you can't last through one hour and ten minutes of church. Please plan ahead.

If your child insists on misbehaving — and what is misbehavior, biblically? Not an example of a particular action, but a principle: you tell them to do something and they don't do it. That's disobedience. You tell them to be quiet and they are not quiet. If your child insists on misbehaving, take them out, address the discipline, and bring them back. Don't keep them out of the service. The worship battle is one worth picking with your children, because it is an eternal battle you are picking with them.

Any questions or comments?

### Making the Sabbath a Delight for Your Children

As parents, we need to help our children make their Sabbath a delight in the Lord. A few things we can do: talk about the sermon with them. It is amazing how much they actually listen. You ask them in the car, "What did you learn in Sunday School?" and they say "I don't know." But as you probe a bit and start listening, they actually learned a lot. Discuss the things that were done and said in church today. Read the Bible to them and let them read to you. Sunday gives you a good opportunity to teach them to pray — let them pray with you.

Sing their favorite Bible songs and hymns. Help them learn the catechism — start with the Children's Catechism and move on. Take them for a walk and talk about God's creation. Play games that are profitable for them. Watch a good video together — not just as a babysitter. Read a good book to them and discuss what God is doing in your own life. Let them learn from your experiences.

The whole book of Ecclesiastes is exactly that — a father and a preacher talking to his children, saying: "Here are the mistakes I made; don't commit them. Here are the good things I did; learn from them." So share your experiences. Let them know how God saved you. Have you ever shared with your children the story of how Christ worked in your life? Involve them in Christian ministry. People talk about family time meaning watching a movie together. But family time can be anything you do together as a family — and many things can be family time.

There are so many things we can do with our children to help them grow up loving the Sabbath. There is really no valid reason why they should grow up resenting the Sabbath day.

Any suggestions from those of you who have practices on the Lord's Day with your family?

*[Katie shares.]*

Let me say this: make sure Dad is involved on Sunday. I'm serious. Dad needs to take the lead on the Sabbath day. He is the one planning these things. He is the one leading the family. In many ways the Sabbath day is just a representation of every other day — and Dad's leadership there matters.

We have come up against the tyranny of the clock again, so I am not going to be able to get to Jerry's question today. Next week, Lord willing, we will address it. But again, brothers and sisters, let's look at this as a blessing from the Lord — a good thing, an opportunity to do all kinds of things that we may not have the opportunity to do on other days of the week, for the glory of God and our own good and blessing.
